scenario: A gardening blogger has multiple drafts and scheduled posts, and wants to quickly check status, channels, and last edited dates before the weekend publishing push.
example_utterances: Can you help me do a quick pre-weekend check of my drafts and scheduled posts?
Find my tomato pruning draft and tell me its status, channels, and last edited date.
What do I already have scheduled for Saturday?
Thanks—skip the suggestions for now.

scenario: Cloud infrastructure team publishing a post-incident review blog post about a major AWS outage that affected their SaaS platform, aimed at their enterprise customers
example_utterances: Give me an outline for the incident review, timeline first then root cause analysis
Write the mitigation steps section based on the outline
Does this read like something we actually wrote or does it sound like a bot
That suggestion doesn't fit our tone, skip it — and tighten up this paragraph, it's too wordy
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: A DevOps engineer at a mid-size enterprise wants to find older internal blog posts about Kubernetes cost optimization before writing a follow-up piece on FinOps practices
example_utterances: Find anything we published on Kubernetes cost optimization
Summarize the top results so I can see what ground we've already covered
What angles on FinOps haven't we touched yet based on what's out there
Show me how the new draft will look before I send it out
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: An enterprise IT team lead is drafting a technical blog post comparing Azure Virtual WAN and traditional hub-and-spoke network topologies for their company blog, targeting network architects
example_utterances: Give me an outline comparing Azure Virtual WAN versus hub-and-spoke for network architects
Adjust the outline — move the routing section earlier and add a trade-offs subsection
The intro paragraph is flat, make it punchier without changing the structure
Write a short summary for the newsletter blurb, then cross-post the full piece to LinkedIn
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: A platform engineering team member is revising a draft blog post about internal developer platforms and wants to make sure it matches the voice of their previous technical posts before sending to the VP for approval
example_utterances: Check the voice against our older technical posts — does this sound like us or does it feel off
The second section is way too formal, shift the tone to match how we usually write
That whole closing paragraph sounds AI-generated, remove it
Once it's clean, schedule it for VP review and flag what still needs sign-off
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: An enterprise IT architect wants a summary of a long-form draft about migrating from VMware vSphere to a cloud-native stack so stakeholders can read it before a strategy meeting
example_utterances: Summarize this VMware migration draft for an exec pre-read, two or three sentences max
Walk me through how you picked what to include in that summary
Start a new post based on this — a shorter stakeholder-facing version for the strategy meeting
Show me how it'll look before I send it out
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: A senior SRE is drafting a blog post about building a self-healing infrastructure using Kubernetes operators and wants a structured outline before writing to ensure full coverage of the topic
example_utterances: Give me an outline for the self-healing infrastructure post — Kubernetes operators, failure scenarios, recovery patterns
Add a section on observability hooks between the operators section and the recovery patterns section
Write the failure scenarios section based on the outline
Compare the recovery patterns section to the previous draft and simplify anything that's gotten too dense
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: A cloud governance team member is polishing a specific paragraph in a blog post about tagging strategies for multi-cloud cost allocation and the sentence structure is awkward and unclear
example_utterances: Find our other posts on cloud cost allocation and tagging strategies
Compare the structure and tone of those against this draft so I know what we're aiming for
This paragraph is clunky — fix the sentence structure without changing what it says
Always keep our tagging posts at a technical but conversational register, save that as a preference
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: Founder revising a draft investor update post — balancing transparency about runway with confidence in growth metrics
example_utterances: Tone down the cash burn part
Highlight our 30% MRR growth more
Add a graph from last month
Too long. Cut by 30%

scenario: Creating a founder story post for Product Hunt launch — personal journey mixed with product benefits
example_utterances: Draft my 'why I built this' section
Make the origin story more emotional
Add tech specs at the end
Shorten the whole thing

scenario: Competitor comparison post — positioning their seed-stage startup against incumbents without naming names
example_utterances: Outline key differences for our pricing page
Less salty tone in section 3
Add a feature matrix
Focus more on speed benefits

scenario: Hiring announcement post — making their first engineering role sound exciting despite limited funding
example_utterances: Make the equity section clearer
Add our weird office perks
Sound less corporate
Shorten the requirements list

scenario: Pivoting announcement — explaining their shift from B2C to B2B without spooking existing users
example_utterances: Help me explain the pivot positively
Assure current users they're not abandoned
Add the new roadmap
Cut the technical reasons section

scenario: Pre-launch teaser post — building anticipation for their closed beta without revealing too much
example_utterances: Write a mysterious tagline
Add the waitlist form link
Make it more urgent
Remove the pricing hints

scenario: Founder responding to a critical tweet thread — turning criticism into a thoughtful blog post opportunity
example_utterances: Summarize the key points from that Twitter thread
How long is this draft compared to our usual posts?
Brainstorm ways to frame this as a positive learning experience
Publish this as a 'lessons learned' post on our blog

scenario: An IT content manager receives a proactive suggestion from Hugo to add an SEO description to a blog post about enterprise service mesh adoption but wants to skip it and move on
example_utterances: Skip that SEO suggestion, not doing it now
Ignore that, what channels are connected for publishing?
Check the status of my service mesh post
Go ahead and schedule it for next Tuesday
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: A cloud infrastructure blogger at an enterprise wants to research what their team has previously written about Terraform state management before drafting a new post on remote backends and state locking
example_utterances: Find everything we've written about Terraform state management
Summarize the most relevant post on IaC state before I start drafting
Start a new post on remote backends and state locking, add a section on locking strategies
Rework the introduction to differentiate it from our previous Terraform coverage
diversity_axis: enterprise IT and cloud infrastructure teams

scenario: A DIY craft blogger keeps ideas in Hugo and wants to browse saved topics and content gaps to pick the next quick weekend project post.
example_utterances: Browse my saved DIY ideas for spring and show me any content gaps in upcycling.
Suggest one quick weekend project topic that fits what I haven’t covered yet.
Brainstorm 5 hook ideas and titles for that project post.
Create the draft, then preview it and help me plan how to promote it once it’s live.

scenario: A book blogger rewrote a review to be less spoilery and wants a highlighted comparison of the revised spoiler section versus the original.
example_utterances: Find the draft of my book review with the spoiler section rewrite.
Diff just the spoiler paragraph (and the last two paragraphs) against the original—highlight additions and cuts.
Polish the revised spoiler section to be less explicit while keeping the tension.
If the rewrite went too far, undo the last edit—then preview the final version.

scenario: A home organization blogger wants Hugo to suggest the next section to improve in a messy draft, but also wants the option to quickly dismiss suggestions while brainstorming.
example_utterances: Audit this messy draft and tell me the biggest issue to fix first.
Suggest one improvement (not a full rewrite) for the next section to tackle.
Nope—dismiss that suggestion and give me a different option.
Outline a cleaner intro structure, then polish just the first paragraph to match it.

scenario: A personal finance hobby blogger is building a monthly budget template post series and wants to compare structure across three posts to keep formatting consistent.
example_utterances: Find my last three monthly budget template posts (January, February, March).
Compare their structure and formatting—are the headings consistent and which one has the densest paragraphs?
Add a citation for the key budgeting guideline I reference in all three posts.
Compose a short standardized 'How to use this template' section, then suggest what to normalize across the series.

scenario: Political Science interaction involving a summary generation followed by setting a default length preference
example_utterances: Summarize the key arguments in this election analysis
Check the metadata to see when this was last updated
Polish the summary to sound more academic
Yes, go ahead and save that length as my default preference

scenario: Anthropologist cleaning up inconsistent heading hierarchy in a field work report
example_utterances: Generate an outline of my field work notes
Brainstorm clear headings for the observation sections
Tidy up the document structure and fix the header hierarchy
Inspect the section word counts to ensure balance

scenario: Early-stage founder drafting a blog post to attract first customers — explaining their unique SaaS solution for small e-commerce businesses
example_utterances: Help me write an intro that hooks small shop owners
Too technical, simplify the how-it-works section
Add a case study from our beta tester
Make the CTA stronger

scenario: A travel hobby blogger is about to schedule a photo-heavy weekend itinerary post and wants to verify metadata like tags, featured image, and scheduled date.
example_utterances: Check this Kyoto weekend itinerary post—does it have tags and a featured image set?
Before I schedule it, tidy the formatting so the photo sections and headings are consistent.
Preview it like it’ll appear when published—especially the image spacing.
Suggest the best publish time for this weekend post, then schedule it.

scenario: A fitness and wellness hobby blogger connects WordPress and Medium, and wants to check publishing channel health after a sync error last week.
example_utterances: Survey my publishing channels—are WordPress and Medium connected and healthy after last week’s sync error?
Can you check whether my latest post is stuck scheduled or partially published anywhere?
If anything is queued on the broken channel, cancel the scheduled publish so it doesn’t fail again.
Once the connections look good, release the post to WordPress and explain what you checked.
